author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: There are lots of different kinds of carbon and they have lots of different properties. For example soot, graphite, diamond are all carbon but physically very different. With graphene you need to keep the conductivity if you used just carbon it wouldn't conduct so you have to mess around with it with all the different chemicals.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I understand from your first message it seemed as if you had a method for graphene synthesis from centrifugation - which seemed a little impossible - My GO has been sitting around for a couple of months now and is still in dispersion. I suggest you sonicate for longer

author: @mojtabasamiee
text: Well, they use centrifuge to separate the unexfoliated GO. I also have tried sonication for my GO dispersion and I still can not get a stable dispersion. maybe I did not try it with high enough power sonication? or not enough time? I have read that monolayer GO should be stable in dispersion for as long as even a year. How long your GO dispersion stays stable? The one I try after a few hours I can see agglomerated GO at the bottom of the container. IS this OK?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Sonication is a pretty good route to graphene - do you have a reference for graphene from centrifugation - I am surprised that is route to graphene and I would be interested to read the article. You are doing nothing wrong. GO and Graphene has a tendency to re-agglomerate unless you disperse with sonication or add a dispersant like PVP

author: @mojtabasamiee
text:
Thank you very much for posting this clip. I am trying to synthesize graphene oxide. In literature, some people have done this with sonication and some have used the centrifuge. I was not sure which one is better? 
The other question I had was about the stability of GO dispersion. Everybody says that GO dispersion is stable for weeks but when I tried to synthesize GO, after a few hours I can see that they start setteling down in the bottom of the container. What am I doing wrong?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Good old Petrik. He's an odd fellow with some controversy surrounding him. He is supposed to be a bit of a russian mafios guy with serious connections in the kremlin and to be a bit of a scam artists. For example he talks about graphite as his precursor (in his patent) but he is clearly using a graphite intercalated compound (GIC) with a catalyst to thermochemically exfoliate the GIC. I have no idea what GIC he is using. But I am currently, with the help of friends, looking into this.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I think they leave something out deliberately so as to make it hard to do. Basically graphite is layer bigger than about 10 layers thick. Few layer graphite (FLG) is 3-10 and graphene is normally 1-3 layers thick. That's true if its graphene or graphene oxide. It needs to be graphine oxide so sonication is need to break the big old clumps of graphite oxide down below the FLG limit in order to make it act like it should

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: To be honest - yes - but then so is driving a car and it's no more dangerous than that with proper precautions. It's probably not something to be attempted as a first project but certainly in the scope of someone with a little experience. What's your background?

text: solubility is about 2-4mg per mL depending on its method of production and therefore the number of attached hydrophillic groups. So it'll be about 1-2g per 500mL. Have yo ul ooked over my graphine part 2 video it really is easy to make. But if you like i'll send you some.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: your right it does - but i got the impression that was only true if you used a solid carbon source. Any how. The graphine forms at lots of different nucleation sites and basically grow until the plates hit each other. So what you get is single sheet of randomly oriented flakes - a bit like a jigsaw and the flake boundaries are weaknesses. I have read that combining the graphene with nanotubules helps over come this weaknessbut so does a few layer thick sheet.

text:
You've caused me to consider the GO paper/Lightscribe method pretty seriously, but ultimately I'd like to get large pieces to make composite foam-epoxy structures.  I noticed that Sony used an electric current to heat sections of a roll up for a 100-meter strip, and that struck me as very elegant.  The trick is to get the copper hot without melting it.  I'm imagining some sort of wiper contacts.

I thought the film was actually applied to the opposite surface before dissolving the copper.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: finally, creating GO paper is pretty easy. Make your GO and vacuum filter it. Dry the filtrate and peel in off the filter paper - hey presto GO paper. I hope all of this helps

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text:
I know of a few approachable ways to get graphene at home these are:
Electrochemical exfoliation
chemical formationof GO and further reduction
mechanical exfoliation (sonication and high energy ball mill)
CVD - of course
chemical growth on a removable substrate.

all this takes loads of time - of course! but what i am trying to do is create a method that anyone can follow at home with readily available chemicals and tools - i think it's possible.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: You should find graphene covering the copper which can be transferred to a plastic film by glueing the copper to the film then etching the copper away with ferric chloride. The tube furnace should be easy enough using a coiled inductance heater. It's the gas flows that are a problem. And don't forget CVD grows wrinkled graphene (due to temp differences in the substrate and graphene sheet. Plus the graphene sheet is prone to cracking.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: then hydrogen flowed over it at 150 mTorr and then heated to 1000 oC (which should take 20 mins) Then hold the furnace at this temp for 15 mins. Then flow methane over it at 6 Torr ofr 13 mins. Then turn the furnace off and let it cool to room temp. When temp reaches 150 oC flow argon over it at 8 Torr. After 2 mins close everything down and let the pressure equalise to room pressure and you are done.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text:
I basically mean to use the growth recipe provided by the McEuen group which is as follows. A piece of  copper foil (0.025mm) has to be cleaned and this is done by dipping in this order

1.Acetone(10seconds)
2.Water(< 10seconds)
3.AceticAcid(10minutes)
4.Water(< 10seconds)
5.Acetone(10seconds)
6.Isopropylalcohol(IPA)(10seconds)

The remaining IPA is removed using compressed air.

Then you can load them into a tube furnace. It needs to be pumped down to 30 mTorr.

author: @voronwae
text: Interesting!  What's your idea for a home setup?  I've been thinking along the lines of a military surplus pressure vessel with a microwave  gun.  I'd planned to use methane, if I can do the whole thing without getting oxygen into it and blowing myself up.  Of course, now you've gotten me thinking about how to deposit your graphite oxide into a paper...

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: You want me to make you some graphene? I have been thinking about a home set up for chemical vapour deposition for a while the problem is the gas flow. The copper deposition is autocatalytic by the way and you can use sugar as your carbon source.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: You want me to make you some graphene? I have been thinking about a home set up for chemical vapour deposition for a while the problem is the gas flow. The copper deposition is autocatalytic by the way and you can use sugar as your carbon source.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I've been thinking about it and I have a few ideas for a home set up for chemical vapour deposition the problem is mostly to do with gas flow. You don't really need to worry about coating the copper substrate as mostly it's auto catalytic but getting the flow of hydrogen argon right is a challenge incidentally you can DVD graphene using sugar.

author: @voronwae
text: I had a very rough time understanding the audio, by the way.  Did you have the gain turned all the way up on your microphone?

author: @voronwae
text:
I'm interested in making sheet graphene, but I'm more interested in making it using copper with propane or methane vapor deposition.  One problem is that  I'd like to get relatively thin layers, and given graphene's transparency I'm not sure how to go about deciding whether I've got a layer.

You mentioned the Lightscribe method, and that makes me wonder whether you might be interested in creating graphene for electronics experimentation.  I'd like high strength and good thermal conductivity.
